items.headline: The Always Offline Founder
items.angle: Mirror the original's direct affirmation style, but shift the aspiration from 'unemployed rich' to the 'always offline' founder. Frame it as the inevitable outcome of building an automated online business that buys back your time for real-world experiences.
hooks: You're going to become that one friend who's always offline.
You're about to become the founder who runs their business from a trailhead.
Soon, your 'Out of Office' will be your permanent status.
You're going to be the person whose best ideas happen a long way from a desk.
Prepare to be the friend who is impossible to reach during the week.
talking_points: You're going to become that one friend who's always offline.
And I don't mean unplugged and broke. I mean thriving.
I mean your business is running, clients are happy, and money is coming in, while you are out in the world, actually living.
Most people think building an online business means being chained to your laptop 24/7.
They trade a cubicle for a digital cage, and that is not the goal.
The real goal is to build intelligent systems once, using automation and AI, so they can work for you forever.
This is how you buy back your time. This is how you get back to the field.
It's about building a business that funds your life, not a business that becomes your life.
If you're ready to build online so you can live offline, I have a clear path for you.
Comment the word 'FieldWork' below, and I'll send you the details on how we do this.

You're going to become that one friend who's always offline. And I don't mean unplugged and broke. I mean thriving. I mean your business is running, clients are being served, and revenue is hitting the bank, all while you are out in the world, actually living your life.

So many people get this wrong. They think building an online business means being chained to a laptop, trading a cubicle for a digital cage. That is not the goal.

The goal is freedom. The strategy is to build intelligent systems once, using simple automation and AI, so they can work for you long after you've closed your computer. guidance: Film this as a simple, direct-to-camera talking head in a natural setting, like by a window with trees outside, or on a porch. Keep the background clean and calm.

items.headline: This Is The Other Game
items.angle: Position the 'Build Online. Live Offline.' philosophy as a conscious choice, an alternative 'game' to the default hustle culture. Frame it not as an escape, but as a deliberate way to engage more deeply with life itself.
hooks: There are two games in life. Most people only know they're playing one.
The rulebook they gave you isn't the only one.
This isn't an escape. It's the whole point.
What if the prize wasn't at the finish line, but on the path?

talking_points: There are two games in life, and most people only know they're playing one.
The first is the one we are all taught. Climb the ladder, accumulate more, and trade your time for a future that might not come.
The scoreboard for that game is your bank account and your title.
But there is another game. The one you play in the field, not just in the office.
Its goal is not just to win. The goal is to play. To spend your time on what actually fills you up.
It is about having slow mornings with your family, afternoons on the trail, and the freedom to be fully present for all of it.
You build the online business so you can truly live offline.
This is not about escaping work. It is about designing your work to serve your life, not the other way around.
If you are ready to learn the rules to that second game, comment 'FIELD' and I will send you the guide to get started.

items.headline: How I Fired Myself To Reclaim My Life
items.angle: Tell the story of your own burnout, the specific moment you realized the 'successful' business you built was a prison, and how you systematically rebuilt it to run without you so you could finally live the 'offline' life you advocate for.
hooks: My business was making seven figures, and I had never been more miserable in my life.
It was a Tuesday in October, and I was staring at a photo of my friends on a mountain I was supposed to be on.
I built a business that was supposed to give me freedom. Instead, it built me a cage.
Here’s the story of how I systematically fired myself from the company I founded.
I had to choose between my business and my life. I almost made the wrong choice.
talking_points: It was a crisp Tuesday in October, and a photo popped up on my phone. It was my best friends on top of a peak we were all supposed to climb together. But I was in my office, chained to a 'critical' client call.
In that moment, I realized the business I’d built to create freedom had done the exact opposite. It was a seven figure cage, and every dollar I made just added another bar.
I was selling this idea of living offline, while being the most terminally online person I knew. I was missing everything that actually mattered.

So I was faced with a choice. Keep grinding, scale the cage, and accept that this was the price of success. Or, burn the entire model to the ground and rebuild it for my life, not just for my bank account.
I chose to burn it down. I went all in on building systems. I learned everything I could about AI and automation to build engines that could run the business without my constant attention.
And it worked. The business runs better now than when I was in it eighty hours a week. More importantly, I haven't missed a trip, a wedding, or a powder day since.
I learned that real wealth isn't your profit margin, it's your time margin. It's about how much life you actually get to live.
This entire experience is why I created FieldWork. It’s the playbook for building an online business that actually gets you offline.
If you want to build a business that serves your life, not the other way around, I can show you how.
Just comment the word 'Field' below and I will send you the details.

items.headline: The Unspoken Founder Question
items.angle: Use the 'Asking for a friend' format to voice the core struggle of founders feeling trapped by their own business. Frame it as a legitimate question before presenting the FieldWork philosophy of building systems as the clear answer.
hooks: Asking for a friend… is it possible to build a great business without sacrificing your entire life for it?
Is it just me, or did we not become founders just to build ourselves a more demanding job?
Real talk: Did anyone else start a business for freedom, only to end up chained to a laptop?
Serious question: Does your business run you, or do you run your business?
I keep hearing founders say this: 'I love my business, but I miss my life.' Sound familiar?
talking_points: Asking for a friend… is it possible to build a successful business without sacrificing your entire life for it?
The hustle culture narrative we are all sold says no. It tells you that more hours and more grind is the only way.
But honestly, that's a broken model. I see so many founders burn out chasing it.
The alternative isn't about working less, it's about working smarter by building systems that work for you, even when you are not.
This means using AI to handle your client intake or setting up automated marketing funnels that run 24/7.
These are not just small tricks. They are the core engines of a business designed to buy back your time.
Time is your most valuable asset, and you should be able to spend it out in the world.
It’s about getting you back to the things that matter, which is probably the reason you started your business in the first place.
If you're ready to build a business that serves your life, not the other way around, comment 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you the details on how we do it.
